old_claim.note: Libraries provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Petrarch's searches of monastic libraries for lost classical manuscripts helped supply early humanism with recoverable classical texts rather than acting as a single required path.

source_shape.source_locator: Classical studies and career section: Britannica says Petrarch searched monastic libraries for lost Classical manuscripts and that these experiences focused his mission as founder of European humanism.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Petrarch's humanist work depended on recovering classical manuscripts through monastic-library searches.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge is enabling because libraries supplied textual access for early humanism without being the only possible route to every humanist work.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: humanism still has libraries as an enabling prerequisite, now with textbook evidence and a scoped note.
would_reject_if: The humanism node is later narrowed to a doctrine independent of classical manuscript recovery.
A stronger source shows Petrarch's library searches were unrelated to his humanist program.
The edge is strengthened to required without evidence that no humanism could emerge outside monastic-library access.
short_reason: Monastic libraries supplied recoverable classical manuscripts for early humanism.
